title: Quick to Listen
reading:
The first instruction James gives is the one most people skip: be quick to listen.

What it really means is this — stop and take in what is happening before you react. Pay attention with your ears and your heart. Try to understand, not defend or escape.

Quick to listen means:
I don't interrupt.
I don't rush to explain myself.
I let the moment settle so truth can be heard.

This is harder than it sounds. Listening is not just staying quiet while you wait for your turn to speak. True listening means you are genuinely trying to understand what the other person is saying — even when it's uncomfortable, even when you disagree.

The opposite of quick to listen shows up in three common ways:
Jumping to conclusions — assuming the worst when the full picture hasn't been seen. Assuming your parents hate you when that's far from the truth.
Being in denial — refusing to accept correction or acknowledge when you're wrong.
Yelling to regain control — getting loud to intimidate someone rather than understand them.

In every one of those opposites, the person has already decided the outcome before hearing the other side. That is the breeding ground for anger. When we stop listening, we stop seeing people clearly — and what fills that gap is usually assumption, fear, or pride.
reflection: Think of a recent conflict. Did you listen to understand, or did you listen to respond? What would have changed if you had slowed down?
abide: Holy Spirit, give me ears to truly hear — not just words, but the hearts behind them. Make me quick to listen today.
